When advising a student about AdvSt Chinese Language and Culture, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. AdvSt Chinese Language and Culture belongs to World Languages. This course is intended for students to expand and deepen their understanding of language structures, vocabulary, applications, and the social and cultural realities of the world in which they live. This is a student-centered, cross-curriculum, project-based class. Topics covered will be relevant to current events and historical and cultural topics in the Mandarin-speaking world. Possible topics for projects include persuasive speech, inter-textual analysis, the Chinese diaspora, the urban and rural divide in China, and attitudes surrounding gastronomy. The course will be enriched by local field studies to such places as LA's Chinatown, Monterey Park and the Claremont College museums where students will engage authentically with language and practice the skills that they have learned in class. The course's communicative approach aims to continue developing students' oral and written proficiency and listening and reading comprehension skills. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing AdvSt Chinese Language and Culture with another Webb class needs more than a label. The advisor should explain that the course appears in World Languages, summarize the actual catalog description, and separate requirements from recommendations. This course is intended for students to expand and deepen their understanding of language structures, vocabulary, applications, and the social and cultural realities of the world in which they live. When advising a student about AdvSt French Language and Culture, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. AdvSt French Language and Culture belongs to World Languages. This French course will guide fourth-year students to explore francophone culture and events in both contemporary and historical settings and to examine products and practices of the target culture. The aim is to promote both fluency and accuracy in language use. The course is conducted entirely in French and will include all aspects of the target language in a variety of settings. Students are expected to express themselves in French, both orally and in writing, coherently, resourcefully, and with reasonable fluency and accuracy. The course is structured around three large units: Families and Communities; Contemporary life; and Identity. Each unit is composed of various themes, and students are encouraged to explore them in various ways. Throughout the course, students will have multiple opportunities for experiential learning and global connections, such as: attending the annual French Film Festival in LA, exploring French/Francophone culinary art in the LA area, participating in virtual exchange/sharing with peers from local and international schools, and learning from prominent francophone professors/experts at local universities. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

Advisors should also help students see how AdvSt French Language and Culture affects the full-year program. A strong plan accounts for five required courses each semester, possible sixth-course choices, afternoon commitments, and the need to keep the schedule challenging but manageable.
When advising a student about AdvSt Mandarin: Stories from Urban & Rural China, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. AdvSt Mandarin: Stories from Urban & Rural China belongs to World Languages. This course invites advanced Mandarin language students to discover the vibrant contrasts between city and country life in China through engaging stories and cultural exploration. This student-centered, interactive course uses accessible Mandarin Companion readers and other adapted materials to bring modern Chinese society to life. Topics covered will explore daily routines, family relationships, education, and lifestyle differences between urban and rural communities. Possible projects include creating digital storytelling videos about a day in the life of a Chinese teenager, comparing school life in Shanghai versus a village school, exploring food culture through family recipes, and discovering how technology shapes modern Chinese life differently in cities and rural areas. The course will be enriched by local field studies to the Claremont Colleges, restaurants, and community centers where students will practice their Mandarin skills in real-world settings and connect with local Mandarin speakers. The course aims to develop students' language abilities through meaningful storytelling, role-play, and cultural comparisons while building a deeper understanding of contemporary Chinese society. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about AdvSt Spanish Language and Culture, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. AdvSt Spanish Language and Culture belongs to World Languages. In this course, students engage in active, real-world communication while developing the ability to understand spoken Spanish in a variety of authentic contexts. Through the study of literature, history, art, and current events, students will develop a strong command of the Spanish language and expand their cultural understanding of the Spanish-speaking world. Course content also includes art, film, literature, songs, and interactive websites with audio/video recordings. This course will focus on student development of proficiency in integrating language skills, synthesizing written and oral material, and being able to comprehend and communicate confidently. Students will be expected to engage in fieldwork, project-based, and multi-disciplinary efforts in this course. Examples of possible projects in the classroom include persuasive essays, inter-textual analysis, Latin-American art project with a field trip to MOLAA museum, visiting the Los Angeles Cathedral designed by Spanish architect Jose Rafael Moneo as part of a field project. The course will be enriched by local field studies to such places as Olvera St. and Spanish cultural events in LA, and our community in Claremont Heritage where students will engage authentically with language and practice the skills that they have learned in class. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing AdvSt Spanish Language and Culture with another Webb class needs more than a label. The advisor should explain that the course appears in World Languages, summarize the actual catalog description, and separate requirements from recommendations. Students will be expected to engage in fieldwork, project-based, and multi-disciplinary efforts in this course. Examples of possible projects in the classroom include persuasive essays, inter-textual analysis, Latin-American art project with a field trip to MOLAA museum, visiting the Los Angeles Cathedral designed by Spanish architect Jose Rafael Moneo as part of a field project. The course will be enriched by local field studies to such places as Olvera St. and Spanish cultural events in LA, and our community in Claremont Heritage where students will engage authentically with language and practice the skills that they have learned in class. A useful next step is to compare this evidence with the student's readiness and goals.
A practical planning conversation about AdvSt Spanish Language and Culture would separate three questions: what the class covers, what preparation it expects, and whether the student's total load is reasonable. In this course, students engage in active, real-world communication while developing the ability to understand spoken Spanish in a variety of authentic contexts. Through the study of literature, history, art, and current events, students will develop a strong command of the Spanish language and expand their cultural understanding of the Spanish-speaking world. Course content also includes art, film, literature, songs, and interactive websites with audio/video recordings. This course will focus on student development of proficiency in integrating language skills, synthesizing written and oral material, and being able to comprehend and communicate confidently. Students will be expected to engage in fieldwork, project-based, and multi-disciplinary efforts in this course. Examples of possible projects in the classroom include persuasive essays, inter-textual analysis, Latin-American art project with a field trip to MOLAA museum, visiting the Los Angeles Cathedral designed by Spanish architect Jose Rafael Moneo as part of a field project. The course will be enriched by local field studies to such places as Olvera St. and Spanish cultural events in LA, and our community in Claremont Heritage where students will engage authentically with language and practice the skills that they have learned in class. Keeping those questions separate prevents catalog facts from turning into generic encouragement.
If AdvSt Spanish Language and Culture appears on a student's possible schedule, the advisor should keep the conversation grounded in Webb's actual course language. In this course, students engage in active, real-world communication while developing the ability to understand spoken Spanish in a variety of authentic contexts. Through the study of literature, history, art, and current events, students will develop a strong command of the Spanish language and expand their cultural understanding of the Spanish-speaking world. Course content also includes art, film, literature, songs, and interactive websites with audio/video recordings. This course will focus on student development of proficiency in integrating language skills, synthesizing written and oral material, and being able to comprehend and communicate confidently. Students will be expected to engage in fieldwork, project-based, and multi-disciplinary efforts in this course. Examples of possible projects in the classroom include persuasive essays, inter-textual analysis, Latin-American art project with a field trip to MOLAA museum, visiting the Los Angeles Cathedral designed by Spanish architect Jose Rafael Moneo as part of a field project. The course will be enriched by local field studies to such places as Olvera St. and Spanish cultural events in LA, and our community in Claremont Heritage where students will engage authentically with language and practice the skills that they have learned in class. The recommendation should mention uncertainty only where the catalog itself does not provide an answer.
When advising a student about AdvSt Spanish Literature and Culture, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. AdvSt Spanish Literature and Culture belongs to World Languages. Students will embark on a literary adventure by reading Spanish works from the 14th to the 21st century. This course challenges the advanced Spanish student with a broad overview of Spanish and Hispanic literature. Students demonstrate their communication skills by analyzing real situations through reading and listening comprehension assessments. In addition to examining and discussing content and stylistic elements, students will explore the historical background of short stories, novels, and poetry. This course provides students with knowledge of literary techniques and builds their literary analysis skills to enhance their understanding of the different cultural components of Spanish literature. The main goal of the course is for students to enjoy the classics of Spanish language literature and to develop their reading, writing, and listening comprehension skills. Examples of possible projects in the classroom include persuasive essays, inter-textual analysis, a "Poems, Rap, music" project with a performance at a local theater and a Mercadillo literario project with the MOLAA museum. The course will be enriched by visits to places such as the San Diego History Center and Spanish Cultural events in LA Renaissance where students will engage authentically with language and practice the skills that they have learned in class. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing AdvSt Spanish Literature and Culture with another Webb class needs more than a label. Advisors should also help students see how AdvSt Spanish Literature and Culture affects the full-year program. A strong plan accounts for five required courses each semester, possible sixth-course choices, afternoon commitments, and the need to keep the schedule challenging but manageable.
When advising a student about Chinese 1, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Chinese 1 belongs to World Languages. This course is an intensive introduction to the Chinese language and culture that builds linguistic proficiency in listening, speaking, reading and writing. Students actively use the target language and explore a diversity of cultures. They acquire language and communicate through the use of a variety of authentic materials such as stories, music, film, art, geography, cuisine and field study. Through interaction with these resources, they learn to compare their lives and cultures with those of their classmates and people around the world. In addition, students develop their skills as independent language learners and global citizens. This course is the first year of a four-year sequence and is for non-native speakers of Chinese with minimal or no background in Mandarin Chinese. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Chinese 2, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Chinese 2 belongs to World Languages. In this course, students continue to raise their ability to communicate in Mandarin Chinese through new vocabulary and grammatical concepts and gain a deeper understanding of Chinese culture as well as Chinese proverbs and idioms. Upon successful completion of this course, students are able to engage in conversations on topics such as their daily schedule, leisure-time activities, school life, describing health issues, asking for directions, purchasing food and making meals and discussing vacation plans. Students are able to read simple texts in Chinese characters with the help of vocabulary lists and are able to write simple dialogues and short paragraphs on familiar topics. Integral to the course are realia, including online media and activities, which play an important role in both language acquisition and cultural awareness. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Chinese 3, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Chinese 3 belongs to World Languages. In this intermediate course, students review past knowledge, learn to use new vocabulary and new grammar structures, explore Chinese literature and mythology, as well as discuss popular topics about modern China through movies and Internet clips. Class activities include group and partner activities, skits, presentations and regular practice with character reading and writing. Upon successful completion of this course, students are able to understand main ideas and basic information from dialogues or public announcements; to glean general ideas from relatively straightforward texts featuring description and narration with the aid of a vocabulary list and to present their ideas/opinions with multiple strategies in oral and written forms. Students will also be able to demonstrate cultural understanding when they communicate. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Chinese 4, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Chinese 4 belongs to World Languages. This course continues the study of Chinese grammar, vocabulary and culture with greater emphasis on written forms and written expository style. The course emphasizes the development of speaking, listening and writing with a particular focus on developing reading skill through the study of short essays, stories and dialogues on Chinese culture and current affairs. Students also explore creative writing. Upon successful completion of this course, students are able to understand short spoken lectures about Chinese society, culture, tradition and current issues. Students are able to orally present personal opinions on more sophisticated topics and to read texts with the help of vocabulary lists on society and culture. Students are able to meet a number of practical writing needs including writing short passages, simple letters, skits, etc. Students also increase familiarity with Chinese Internet websites, online dictionaries and word-processing. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing Chinese 4 with another Webb class needs more than a label. The advisor should explain that the course appears in World Languages, summarize the actual catalog description, and separate requirements from recommendations. Students also explore creative writing. Upon successful completion of this course, students are able to understand short spoken lectures about Chinese society, culture, tradition and current issues. Students are able to orally present personal opinions on more sophisticated topics and to read texts with the help of vocabulary lists on society and culture. Students are able to meet a number of practical writing needs including writing short passages, simple letters, skits, etc. Students also increase familiarity with Chinese Internet websites, online dictionaries and word-processing.
If a student is interested in Chinese 4, a balanced advising note would begin with the listed course information. The next move is to ask whether the student's current preparation, schedule, and non-academic commitments make the course appropriately challenging.

When advising a student about Chinese 5, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Chinese 5 belongs to World Languages. The World Language Department is committed to offering a fifth year of study for students who enter at level 2 or higher. Depending on enrollment, these courses may be offered through an online partnership or through independent study. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about French 1, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. French 1 belongs to World Languages. This course is an intensive introduction to the French language and Francophone cultures that builds linguistic proficiency in listening, speaking, reading and writing. Students actively use the target language and explore a diversity of cultures. They acquire language and communicate through the use of a variety of authentic materials such as stories, music, film, art, geography and cuisine. Through interaction with these resources, they learn to compare their lives and cultures with those of their classmates and people around the world. In addition, students develop their skills as independent language learners and global citizens. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

Advisors should also help students see how French 1 affects the full-year program. A strong plan accounts for five required courses each semester, possible sixth-course choices, afternoon commitments, and the need to keep the schedule challenging but manageable.
When advising a student about French 2, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. French 2 belongs to World Languages. Taught in French, this course is for students who are familiar with and can express their ideas using different verb tenses, understand native French spoken at a moderate pace, and can make themselves understood in a variety of situations. Students experience different aspects of French culture throughout the year, including French cuisine and films; they also complete a short reader Destination France as an introduction to French literature. Integral to the course are authentic materials, including media available over the Internet, which play an important role in both language acquisition and cultural awareness. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about French 3, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. French 3 belongs to World Languages. Taught in French, this course is not only an overview of French and Francophone cultures, but also a journey to the dawn of life on our planet. Class activities are in association with the Raymond M. Alf Museum of Paleontology and the Lycee International de Los Angeles, a French-speaking elementary school in nearby Pasadena. The goals of the course include giving oral presentations about Webb's paleontology museum and creating written material in French related to paleontology. This course includes a general review of French grammar, and students engage in group discussions as well as partner and individual activities to actively practice communication. Authentic texts and videos also complement the curriculum. In addition, students read, research and present about contemporary topics using various media, including Internet sites, to describe the many different cultures of the French-speaking world. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing French 3 with another Webb class needs more than a label. When advising a student about French 4, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. French 4 belongs to World Languages. Taught in French as a continuation of French 3, this course is not only an overview of French and Francophone cultures, but also a journey to the dawn of life on our planet. Class activities are in association with the Raymond M. Alf Museum of Paleontology and the Lycee International de Los Angeles, a French-speaking elementary school in nearby Pasadena. The goals of the course include leading tours and mentoring other students in creating and giving oral presentations in French about Webb's paleontology museum. Students also produce advanced-level written material in French related to paleontology. This course includes a general review of more advanced French grammar, and students take the lead in group discussions to actively practice communication skills. In addition, students read, research and present about contemporary topics using various media, including Internet sites, to describe the many different cultures of the French-speaking world. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

Students also produce advanced-level written material in French related to paleontology. This course includes a general review of more advanced French grammar, and students take the lead in group discussions to actively practice communication skills. In addition, students read, research and present about contemporary topics using various media, including Internet sites, to describe the many different cultures of the French-speaking world.

Class activities are in association with the Raymond M. Alf Museum of Paleontology and the Lycee International de Los Angeles, a French-speaking elementary school in nearby Pasadena. The goals of the course include leading tours and mentoring other students in creating and giving oral presentations in French about Webb's paleontology museum. Students also produce advanced-level written material in French related to paleontology. This course includes a general review of more advanced French grammar, and students take the lead in group discussions to actively practice communication skills. In addition, students read, research and present about contemporary topics using various media, including Internet sites, to describe the many different cultures of the French-speaking world. The explanation can then connect the course to interests, current performance, teacher feedback, and the rest of the yearly schedule.
For French 4, good advising language is direct and evidence-based. The course is in World Languages, and the catalog gives a concrete description rather than a vague subject label. Taught in French as a continuation of French 3, this course is not only an overview of French and Francophone cultures, but also a journey to the dawn of life on our planet. Advisors should also help students see how French 4 affects the full-year program. A strong plan accounts for five required courses each semester, possible sixth-course choices, afternoon commitments, and the need to keep the schedule challenging but manageable.
When advising a student about Hon French 2, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Hon French 2 belongs to World Languages. Taught in French, this accelerated course is for students with high proficiency in all four skill areas of listening, speaking, reading and writing. At the honors level there is a greater emphasis not only on writing and the mastery of complex grammatical concepts, but also on oral fluency and listening skills. Students are also exposed to authentic online materials to gain a better understanding of cultural concepts. In addition to the technology-based textbook and the online interactive program, students read authentic French texts and complete a reader Destination France as an introduction to literature. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Hon French 3, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Hon French 3 belongs to World Languages. Taught in French, this course is designed for highly motivated students who have demonstrated a commitment to and interest in mastering their communication skills in French. The fast-paced curriculum offers a challenging approach to the four language skills: listening, reading, speaking and writing. The course focuses on both language acquisition and cultural knowledge, and students are introduced to the diverse cultures of Francophone countries around the world. In addition, students read French literature such as Le Petit Prince and listen to authentic online resources while integrating information on the arts, history and current events. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Hon Spanish 2, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Hon Spanish 2 belongs to World Languages. Taught in Spanish, this accelerated course is for students with high proficiency in all four skill areas of speaking, listening, reading and writing. At the honors level there is a greater emphasis not only on writing and the mastery of complex grammatical concepts, but also on oral fluency and listening skills. In addition to working with the textbook and the interactive online program, students are introduced to authentic Spanish literature such as El Quijote and Leyendas latinoamericanas to improve their reading skills and cultural knowledge. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Hon Spanish 3, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Hon Spanish 3 belongs to World Languages. Taught in Spanish, this course is designed for highly motivated students who have demonstrated the commitment and interest to master their communication skills in Spanish. The fast-paced curriculum offers a challenging approach to the four language skills: listening, speaking, reading and writing. Grammar and vocabulary are covered in greater depth while students continue to develop their understanding of Spanish-speaking cultures by integrating information on art, history, current events and reading pieces of literature such as the works of Gabriel Garcia Marquez, Carlos Fuentes, Julio Cortazar, Isabel Allende and Jorge Luis Borges. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Spanish 1, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Spanish 1 belongs to World Languages. This course is an intensive introduction to the Spanish language and Hispanic cultures that builds linguistic proficiency in listening, speaking, reading and writing. Students actively use the target language and explore a diversity of cultures. They acquire language and communicate through the use of a variety of authentic materials such as stories, music, film, art, geography and cuisine. Through interaction with these resources, they learn to compare their lives and cultures with those of their classmates and people around the world. In addition, students develop their skills as independent language learners and global citizens. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Spanish 2, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Spanish 2 belongs to World Languages. Taught in Spanish in a blended format, this course is for novice students who are familiar with and can express their ideas using different verb tenses and are able to produce simple sentences on familiar topics. Students interact solely in the target language, interpret authentic Spanish texts, including maps, menus and music by Los Aterciopelados and Calle 13. They also create engaging multimedia presentations, including virtual tours of regional Spanish culture and environmentally-conscious Public Service Announcements. These collaborative, digital projects play an important role in both language acquisition and cultural awareness. By the end of this course, students can use Spanish to narrate experiences, speak about hopes/desires and connect to real and hypothetical events, whether in the past, present or future. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing Spanish 2 with another Webb class needs more than a label. The advisor should explain that the course appears in World Languages, summarize the actual catalog description, and separate requirements from recommendations. By the end of this course, students can use Spanish to narrate experiences, speak about hopes/desires and connect to real and hypothetical events, whether in the past, present or future.
If a student is interested in Spanish 2, a balanced advising note would begin with the listed course information. Taught in Spanish in a blended format, this course is for novice students who are familiar with and can express their ideas using different verb tenses and are able to produce simple sentences on familiar topics. Students interact solely in the target language, interpret authentic Spanish texts, including maps, menus and music by Los Aterciopelados and Calle 13. They also create engaging multimedia presentations, including virtual tours of regional Spanish culture and environmentally-conscious Public Service Announcements. These collaborative, digital projects play an important role in both language acquisition and cultural awareness. By the end of this course, students can use Spanish to narrate experiences, speak about hopes/desires and connect to real and hypothetical events, whether in the past, present or future. The next move is to ask whether the student's current preparation, schedule, and non-academic commitments make the course appropriately challenging.

Advisors should also help students see how Spanish 2 affects the full-year program. A strong plan accounts for five required courses each semester, possible sixth-course choices, afternoon commitments, and the need to keep the schedule challenging but manageable.
When advising a student about Spanish 3, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Spanish 3 belongs to World Languages. Taught in Spanish in a blended format, this intermediate-level course takes a collaborative approach to expanding students' communication skills and cultural knowledge. This project-driven course combines a general review of Spanish grammar and thematic vocabulary building, which inform student-produced multi-media presentations, including public service announcements, travel advertisements and zany infomercials. Students explore the cultural products, practices, and personalities of Latin America and Spain through a variety of digital and hands-on formats. By the end of this course, students will have gained knowledge of political and social issues relevant to the Spanish-speaking world, while building confidence and fluency interacting, interpreting, and presenting on a variety of familiar topics. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing Spanish 3 with another Webb class needs more than a label. The advisor should explain that the course appears in World Languages, summarize the actual catalog description, and separate requirements from recommendations. When advising a student about Spanish 4, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Spanish 4 belongs to World Languages. Throughout this course students will apply the skills that they have mastered in their previous study of the Spanish language. This course is designed to help students achieve a greater ability to communicate in Spanish, to read with minimum use of a dictionary and to write well-organized and logically developed essays with a good command of grammar and vocabulary. This learning is reinforced through explanations of the concepts, exercises, grammar-oriented situations, and writing activities in and out of class. In addition, cultural elements, music, reading and listening comprehension activities and video clips about such topics as the socio-economic impact of immigration in Peru and Mexico are implemented to enhance students' grasp of vocabulary and culture and provide opportunities to express themselves in Spanish. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Advanced Art, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Advanced Art belongs to Fine Arts. This course continues the exploration of materials, techniques and visual strategies begun in Art AB classes, but with an emphasis on developing the student's individual relationship to their artistic process. In the first part of the year, students explore how and why we use art to make representational images of the world around us and how representational skills can be utilized for expressive intent. Through drawing and painting from direct observation, students develop an ability to represent three-dimensional space on a two-dimensional surface in a range of drawing and painting media (graphite, charcoal, watercolor, acrylic, oil, etc). In the second part of the year, students are challenged to cultivate their own artistic voice and prepare to begin autonomous art making. To achieve this, students develop advanced-level projects that explore themes examined by contemporary artists today. Projects are made in a variety of media and new art forms like installation, and participatory practice is introduced. This course is open to students who have taken Art AB or with departmental approval. Upon completing this course, students may apply for Honors Studio Art. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing Advanced Art with another Webb class needs more than a label. When advising a student about AdvSt Performance in Context, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. AdvSt Performance in Context belongs to Fine Arts. This course will be offered in the 2027-2028 academic year. In this course, students regularly step beyond campus to explore the performing arts through the dynamic cultural landscape of Los Angeles. Students investigate the history and ideas behind performances and exhibitions before experiencing them live, learning how preparation can transform what it means to be a creator and consumer of art. Dynamic projects, reflective writing, and a creative capstone project invite students to artistically respond to their experiences. Students will be expected to attend live performances outside the academic day (Friday/Saturday nights, Saturday/Sunday afternoons) as attendance is an essential part of the course work. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing AdvSt Performance in Context with another Webb class needs more than a label. If AdvSt Performance in Context appears on a student's possible schedule, the advisor should keep the conversation grounded in Webb's actual course language. This course will be offered in the 2027-2028 academic year. In this course, students regularly step beyond campus to explore the performing arts through the dynamic cultural landscape of Los Angeles. Students investigate the history and ideas behind performances and exhibitions before experiencing them live, learning how preparation can transform what it means to be a creator and consumer of art. Dynamic projects, reflective writing, and a creative capstone project invite students to artistically respond to their experiences. Students will be expected to attend live performances outside the academic day (Friday/Saturday nights, Saturday/Sunday afternoons) as attendance is an essential part of the course work. The recommendation should mention uncertainty only where the catalog itself does not provide an answer.
When advising a student about Art AB, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Art AB belongs to Fine Arts. Art AB is an introductory art course, designed to acquaint students with the processes, materials and techniques for creating works of visual art. Emphasis is placed on hands-on experiences with diverse two and three dimensional art materials as well as through engagements with finished works of art. The journey of learning in this course follows a pattern of serious play. Each lesson invites students to explore, be aware, transform, give meaning, communicate and reflect on experiences working with, shaping and reshaping materials. This course establishes a creative foundation for students as they develop the ability and confidence to give meaning to material. At the end of this course, students will be eligible for Advanced Art. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Filmmaking Studio I, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Filmmaking Studio I belongs to Fine Arts. In this course, students will learn videography techniques for short format, commercial and narrative visual story telling. The first semester will allow students to explore and engage with the fundamentals of framing, composition rules, camera techniques, lighting, audio, rigging and editing. During the second semester, students will produce, create and shoot a short film all the way from pre- to post-production; through planning, documentation, logistical techniques, scriptwriting basics, cinematography and story creation skills. Students will also learn the basics of distribution and marketing in the film industry and explore career opportunities. Leveraging our proximity to Los Angeles and Hollywood, multiple field studies experiences will help students to get an inside look into the film industry. By the end of the course, students will have established a solid foundation for a film portfolio. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

Advisors should also help students see how Filmmaking Studio I affects the full-year program. A strong plan accounts for five required courses each semester, possible sixth-course choices, afternoon commitments, and the need to keep the schedule challenging but manageable.
When advising a student about Filmmaking Studio II, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Filmmaking Studio II belongs to Fine Arts. This course is an advanced, project-based film production course designed for students who are ready to refine their technical, analytical, and creative skills in cinematic storytelling. Through hands-on studio practice, students deepen their understanding of cinematography, directing, production design, sound design, and post-production workflows while engaging with film as both an artistic and cultural medium. Students explore how visual language communicates complex emotional, psychological, and thematic ideas and apply these techniques to create original, polished films that reflect diverse perspectives and contemporary issues. This course emphasizes creative independence, professional workflow, and real-world problem-solving. Students will collaborate in production teams, pitch and defend artistic choices, and develop a personal visual style while producing multiple short-form projects. This course demands advanced creative rigor, critical thinking, leadership, and reflective practice. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Hon Chamber Singers, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Hon Chamber Singers belongs to Fine Arts. In this course, students continue to work on vocal technique and interpretation as well as exploring ensemble singing of all styles and periods. In addition to live singing, students learn about studio performance, and have the opportunity to record their work in the Digital Music Studio. Performance is an essential component of this course, so participants are expected to present ensemble and solo work in seasonal concerts, recitals and campus events. This course may be repeated for credit with departmental approval and is open to students with departmental approval after Vocal Training & Techniques, or by audition. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Hon Sinfonia Orchestra: String, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Hon Sinfonia Orchestra: String belongs to Fine Arts. Honors Sinfonia is open by audition and requires departmental approval for enrollment. It is designed for advanced musicians who have demonstrated exceptional performance skills and musicianship. Honors students are expected to memorize and play 12 major scales, participate in peer mentoring during labs and rehearsals, and take on sectional leadership positions. Participants also have the opportunity to prepare independent repertoire for public performance. This course may be repeated for credit with departmental approval. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

Advisors should also help students see how Hon Sinfonia Orchestra: String affects the full-year program. A strong plan accounts for five required courses each semester, possible sixth-course choices, afternoon commitments, and the need to keep the schedule challenging but manageable.
When advising a student about Hon Sinfonia Orchestra: Wind & Percussion, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Hon Sinfonia Orchestra: Wind & Percussion belongs to Fine Arts. Honors Sinfonia is open by audition and requires departmental approval for enrollment. It is designed for advanced musicians who have demonstrated exceptional performance skills and musicianship. Honors students are expected to memorize and play 12 major scales, participate in peer mentoring during labs and rehearsals, and take on sectional leadership positions. Participants also have the opportunity to prepare independent repertoire for public performance. This course may be repeated for credit with departmental approval. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Hon Studio Art Digital, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Hon Studio Art Digital belongs to Fine Arts. This course is a guided studio exploration for the advanced artist who is ready for the rigor of independent art making. Candidates submit a proposal at the beginning of the year that is considered by the Arts faculty. Once approved, students are responsible for creating a schedule of work and goals, which are supervised by a member of the Arts faculty. Each student is given individual feedback and support throughout the year. By the end of each semester, students will have produced a cohesive body of work along with an artist statement and a better understanding of their own artistic process. This course is open to students who have taken Advanced Art or New Media Culture & Communication and receive departmental approval to enroll. It may be repeated for credit with departmental approval. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Hon Theater Performance and Production, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Hon Theater Performance and Production belongs to Fine Arts. This course is process-based and experiential, incorporating performance, technology and design. First semester modules will include improvisation, stage combat, musical theater, and acting for camera. Off stage, students will get hands-on experience learning the basics of lighting, sound, prop making and costuming. The second semester will focus on design and production, including units on directing, playwriting, lighting and set design. Each semester will culminate in either a performance or production showcase. Prerequisites: Theater Arts 1-2 or previous participation in a Webb play or musical (performance or tech). The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Media Arts, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Media Arts belongs to Fine Arts. Students in this class begin exploring digital artmaking tools such as Photoshop, TinkerCAD, AfterEffects and GameMaker to learn how to make a variety of digital media, such as illustration, graphic design, animation, game design and 3D modeling. In addition to learning the basics of digital artmaking, students begin to establish their online identity by learning to cultivate a professional social media presence and by learning to maintain website portfolios. No prior knowledge is needed, and artists of all skill levels are welcome, as the class is designed to meet the needs of beginners as well as experts. At the end of this class, students will be eligible for New Media Culture & Communication. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about New Media Culture & Communication, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. New Media Culture & Communication belongs to Fine Arts. In this course, students continue to explore a variety of artmaking software, deepening their understanding of tools such as Photoshop, Illustrator, Blender and GameMaker. In addition to learning advanced techniques for illustration, graphic design, 3D modeling, animation and game design, students plan, produce and present independent projects in the media of their choice, allowing greater opportunities to specialize in a particular area of interest and preparing students for the rigors of Honors Studio Art. Finally, students continue to explore online art communities through various social media and expand their online presence by maintaining portfolio websites. Open to students who have taken Media Arts or with departmental approval, this course is intended for artists who wish to both broaden their repertoire of digital artmaking skills and to independently deepen their expertise in a specific media of choice. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing New Media Culture & Communication with another Webb class needs more than a label. The advisor should explain that the course appears in Fine Arts, summarize the actual catalog description, and separate requirements from recommendations. In this course, students continue to explore a variety of artmaking software, deepening their understanding of tools such as Photoshop, Illustrator, Blender and GameMaker. In addition to learning advanced techniques for illustration, graphic design, 3D modeling, animation and game design, students plan, produce and present independent projects in the media of their choice, allowing greater opportunities to specialize in a particular area of interest and preparing students for the rigors of Honors Studio Art. Finally, students continue to explore online art communities through various social media and expand their online presence by maintaining portfolio websites. Open to students who have taken Media Arts or with departmental approval, this course is intended for artists who wish to both broaden their repertoire of digital artmaking skills and to independently deepen their expertise in a specific media of choice.
If a student is interested in New Media Culture & Communication, a balanced advising note would begin with the listed course information. The next move is to ask whether the student's current preparation, schedule, and non-academic commitments make the course appropriately challenging.

Advisors should also help students see how New Media Culture & Communication affects the full-year program. A strong plan accounts for five required courses each semester, possible sixth-course choices, afternoon commitments, and the need to keep the schedule challenging but manageable.
When advising a student about Sinfonia Orchestra: String, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Sinfonia Orchestra: String belongs to Fine Arts. In this performance-based course, intermediate-to-advanced musicians improve their technical skills, deepen their musical understanding, and develop their musicianship. Participants perform with The Webb Schools Orchestra, Strings Ensemble and small chamber groups and special events throughout the year. This course may be repeated for credit with departmental approval. Students may audition to advance to Honors Sinfonia Orchestra in subsequent years. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing Sinfonia Orchestra: String with another Webb class needs more than a label. When advising a student about Sinfonia Orchestra: Wind & Percussion, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Sinfonia Orchestra: Wind & Percussion belongs to Fine Arts. In this performance-based course, intermediate-to-advanced wind players and percussionists improve their technical skills, deepen their musical understanding and develop their musicianship. Participants perform with The Webb Schools Orchestra, Winds Ensemble and small chamber groups and special events throughout the year. This course may be repeated for credit with departmental approval. Students may audition to advance to Honors Sinfonia Orchestra in subsequent years. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Theater Arts 1-2, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Theater Arts 1-2 belongs to Fine Arts. This course is designed to introduce students to a variety of performance skills and techniques, theatrical genres and theater terminology. Physical and vocal exercises give students the skills they need to move, speak and think creatively on the stage and in the classroom. Performance techniques are introduced and discovered by exploring different types of theater, including improvisation, mask work, devised theater, Ancient Greek and Commedia d'ell Arte. Students are also encouraged to create and develop their own performances, devise characters for monologues and scene work and produce their own one-act plays. This class is a prerequisite for Shakespeare Through Performance and Performance Workshop. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about AdvSt Calculus I, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. AdvSt Calculus I belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. This college-level course gives students an in-depth treatment of limits, derivatives, continuity, and integration. Students are expected to move quickly beyond computational proficiency to achieve conceptual and theoretical understanding. Using a student-centered approach to problem solving, this course relies on the students' ability to work both collaboratively as well as independently to explore these concepts. Heavy emphasis is placed on connecting algebraic, numeric, graphical, and verbal representations of functions. Technology and on-line resources (graphing calculators, GeoGebra, and Desmos) are essential tools for examining properties of various functions. This course is a pre-requisite for Advanced Studies Calculus II. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about AdvSt Calculus II, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. AdvSt Calculus II belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. This rigorous college-level course builds upon the conceptual and theoretical foundations students developed in Advanced Studies Calculus I. Concepts are often explored using technology (such as with graphing calculators, Desmos, and GeoGebra), followed by algebraic proof. Emphasis is placed on developing strong communication skills: clearly presenting methods, reasoning, justifications, and conclusions using accurate and precise mathematical language and notation. Students will also gain confidence and fluency in proving the major theorems of one-variable calculus. Major topics include: limits and continuous functions, epsilon-delta proofs, L'Hopital's rule, advanced techniques of integration, Euler's method, logistic growth, improper integrals, parametric and polar functions, vectors, sequences and series, Taylor polynomials, and power series. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing AdvSt Calculus II with another Webb class needs more than a label. The advisor should explain that the course appears in Mathematics & Computer Science, summarize the actual catalog description, and separate requirements from recommendations. For AdvSt Calculus II, good advising language is direct and evidence-based. The course is in Mathematics & Computer Science, and the catalog gives a concrete description rather than a vague subject label. This rigorous college-level course builds upon the conceptual and theoretical foundations students developed in Advanced Studies Calculus I. Concepts are often explored using technology (such as with graphing calculators, Desmos, and GeoGebra), followed by algebraic proof. Emphasis is placed on developing strong communication skills: clearly presenting methods, reasoning, justifications, and conclusions using accurate and precise mathematical language and notation. Students will also gain confidence and fluency in proving the major theorems of one-variable calculus. Major topics include: limits and continuous functions, epsilon-delta proofs, L'Hopital's rule, advanced techniques of integration, Euler's method, logistic growth, improper integrals, parametric and polar functions, vectors, sequences and series, Taylor polynomials, and power series. A useful next step is to compare this evidence with the student's readiness and goals.
A practical planning conversation about AdvSt Calculus II would separate three questions: what the class covers, what preparation it expects, and whether the student's total load is reasonable. When advising a student about AdvSt Linear Algebra, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. AdvSt Linear Algebra belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. This course uses the language of matrices and vectors to explore the relationship between algebraic and geometric thinking in the plane and in three-space, and then ultimately extends those same ideas into higher dimensions. Topics will include vector geometry, systems of linear equations, matrix algebra, geometric transformations, vector spaces, linear mapping, change of basis, eigenvalues and eigenvectors. In order to strike a balance between theory and practice, application modules will be interwoven throughout the course. These may include Linear Programming, Markov Chains, Computer Graphics, Recursions, and Quadratic Forms. Computer software and the TI-Nspire calculator will be used extensively. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing AdvSt Linear Algebra with another Webb class needs more than a label. When advising a student about AdvSt Multivariable Calculus, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. AdvSt Multivariable Calculus belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. Multivariable Calculus is recommended for students with strong backgrounds in the problem-solving aspects of one-variable calculus who require a rigorous treatment of calculus in several dimensions. Both the theoretical and problem-solving aspects of multivariable calculus are treated carefully. Topics examined include parametric equations and polar coordinates, vectors in two and three-dimensional Euclidean spaces, differentiation in R^n (including partial derivatives, gradients, the total derivative, the Chain Rule, optimization problems and vector-valued functions), and integration in R^n (including Fubini's Theorem and iterated integration, line and surface integrals, differential forms and the theorems of Green, Gauss and Stokes). Additional topics, such as the topology of R^n including the Bolzano-Weierstrass and Heine-Borel theorems, and a treatment of abstract metric spaces, compact sets and continuous mappings, may be introduced as time permits to accommodate student interests. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about AdvSt Statistics, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. AdvSt Statistics belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. Advanced Studies Statistics is a rigorous seminar in applied statistics. We begin by examining the topics of central tendency and variation, data displays, and probability. This leads to the study of inferential statistical topics that include the concepts of statistical models and use of samples, variation, statistical measures, sampling distributions, probability theory, tests of significance, one-way and factorial analysis of variance and covariance and elementary experimental design, multiple linear regression and correlational design, and chi-square. Students will be expected to critically analyze quantitative research, evaluate the evidence on which generalizations are made, collect data, and analyze professional data sets. They present their findings orally and in writing. In addition, students will learn to code using an industry standard statistical package. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Algebra 1, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Algebra 1 belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. This course provides a thorough study of variables and variation, requiring students to use order of operations, like terms and mathematical properties to simplify expressions as well as to develop equations. Thinking about ratios, proportions and percentage leads naturally to the study of direct and inverse variation, rates of change and slope. A problem-based learning approach is used to introduce new topics; students focus on word problems and the ability to critically read and analyze challenging problems in which mathematical skills and concepts are introduced and developed in context rather than strictly from simple drill and practice. Student engagement is encouraged in the classroom by incorporating cooperative learning activities, as well as individual and group presentations of rich, multi-step problems. Topics covered include: equations and graphs that are linear and quadratic, distinguishing linear versus nonlinear data, inequalities, the basic rules of exponents, factoring, unit conversions and other traditional Algebra I topics. A successful completion of the course ensures students' preparation for the next course in the math sequence, Integrated Mathematics I. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Calculus, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Calculus belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. This first course in calculus gives students an in-depth treatment of limits, continuity and derivatives, along with an introduction to integrals. An applied calculus approach introduces concepts with real-world problems related to topics such as economics, medicine, and forensics. Limits are studied in such a way that students see the relationship between slope, the limit concept, and the derivative. Emphasis is placed on applications of derivatives and integrals and the interconnection between analytical, graphical and tabular representations of functions. Tools used to accentuate the learning environment include the TI-Nspire graphing calculator, as well as on-line resources; these are used extensively for examining the properties of functions, limits, derivatives and integrals. A culminating project requires students to apply derivatives and integrals to real-world applications as well as to present the project in a professional format using current technology/media. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing Calculus with another Webb class needs more than a label. The advisor should explain that the course appears in Mathematics & Computer Science, summarize the actual catalog description, and separate requirements from recommendations. When advising a student about HMC/Webb Adv Computer Science A (Fall), the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. HMC/Webb Adv Computer Science A (Fall) belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. Advanced courses in Computer Science are offered in partnership with faculty from Harvey Mudd College. Students may take CS5, HMCs introductory course. From there, they have access to other advanced offerings. Harvey Mudd faculty and students support Webb students in their studies, both on our campus and at HMC's facilities. Specific course offerings are dependent on interest, and the schedule with HMC is worked out each spring. Students may apply for consideration to enroll in our HMC partnership program in writing through the chair of Mathematics & Computer Science. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Hon Integrated Math II (Alg II/Trig), the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Hon Integrated Math II (Alg II/Trig) belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. In this course, students examine all the topics found in Integrated Mathematics II, but in greater depth, and with an emphasis on tackling more challenging problems and applications. The mechanics of mathematical modeling is introduced and practiced, with special attention placed on its importance across academic disciplines, especially science. High demands are placed on the students algebraic techniques and their ability to think both creatively and independently, as well as on their facility to use the TI-Nspire graphing calculator, along with various on line and computer software such as GeoGebra, to uncover the underlying concepts. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Hon Number Theory, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Hon Number Theory belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. How and why did humans develop mathematics, and how has it been used in cultures around the world? This course takes a hands-on approach to exploring the development of mathematics from ancient to modern times. Elementary number theory is taught through the lens of its historic context and through classic math puzzles and problems such as the Sieve of Eratosthenes and squaring the circle. Topics covered in the course include number systems and arithmetic in ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia, logic, proofs, and geometric constructions in ancient Greece, Diophantine equations, proofs of the Pythagorean theorem, the Fibonacci sequence, the Golden Ratio, primality testing, and historical ciphers. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Hon Precalculus, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Hon Precalculus belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. Students examine all of the topics in Precalculus, but in greater depth and with an eye toward application to other fields of study. In addition, students are exposed to ideas and topics not ordinarily seen in a Precalculus course, such as fractals, different levels of infinity, graph theory, probability, cryptography and number theory. Considerable emphasis is placed on developing concise and beautiful mathematical arguments. Computer software and the TI-Nspire graphing calculator allow students to fit functions to data and to explore graphical representations of mathematical models. This course provides students with a rigorous preparation for the subsequent study of AdvSt Calculus I. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing Hon Precalculus with another Webb class needs more than a label. The advisor should explain that the course appears in Mathematics & Computer Science, summarize the actual catalog description, and separate requirements from recommendations. Students examine all of the topics in Precalculus, but in greater depth and with an eye toward application to other fields of study. In addition, students are exposed to ideas and topics not ordinarily seen in a Precalculus course, such as fractals, different levels of infinity, graph theory, probability, cryptography and number theory. Considerable emphasis is placed on developing concise and beautiful mathematical arguments. Computer software and the TI-Nspire graphing calculator allow students to fit functions to data and to explore graphical representations of mathematical models. This course provides students with a rigorous preparation for the subsequent study of AdvSt Calculus I.
If a student is interested in Hon Precalculus, a balanced advising note would begin with the listed course information. The next move is to ask whether the student's current preparation, schedule, and non-academic commitments make the course appropriately challenging.

When advising a student about Integrated Math I (Geom/Alg II), the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Integrated Math I (Geom/Alg II) belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. This course provides an in-depth study of geometry while intentionally integrating appropriate algebraic processes. Using a problem-based learning approach, geometry topics such as lines, segments and polygons, and the properties of angles, triangles, similarities and congruence are covered through a rigorous application of coordinate geometry and vectors. An exploration of linear motion is begun through the introduction of parameters and the investigation of optimal paths of travel. A heavy emphasis on transformations leads to a less static vision of geometry. Topics also include right triangle trigonometry, circles and parabolas introduced from a coordinate geometry perspective. The TI-Nspire graphing calculator, along with computer software such as GeoGebra, is used to analyze data and promote analytical thinking skills. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing Integrated Math I (Geom/Alg II) with another Webb class needs more than a label. When advising a student about Integrated Math II (Alg II/Trig), the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Integrated Math II (Alg II/Trig) belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. The purpose of Integrated Mathematics II is to enable students to expand their view of algebra and geometry to include nonlinear motion and nonlinear functions. Using a problem-based learning approach, the investigation includes quadratic functions and their applications, and circular motion with an emphasis on the ensuing trigonometric functions. The study of vectors and transformations begun in Integrated Mathematics I is expanded upon in this investigation of the trigonometric functions and their properties. In preparation for Precalculus, exponential and logarithmic relationships are introduced. The TI-Nspire graphing calculator, along with various on-line and computer software such as GeoGebra, is used to gain insight into the characteristics of the functions studied and to promote analytical thinking skills. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Precalculus, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Precalculus belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. In Precalculus, students thoroughly examine the gallery of basic functions, which include linear, quadratic, exponential, logarithmic, trigonometric, polynomial and rational. Using a problem-based learning approach, students solve problems and apply real-world data using graphical, numerical and analytical approaches. This course includes a comprehensive study of circular and analytic trigonometry and concludes with an extension of sequences, series and asymptotic behavior. Students utilize computer software, such as GeoGebra, and the TI-Nspire graphing calculator to analyze data, investigate graphs and develop mathematical models. Precalculus ensures students' preparation for the next major step in the mathematics curriculum, Calculus or AdvSt Calculus 1. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Statistics, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Statistics belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. This first course in probability and statistics involves students in collecting, analyzing and drawing conclusions about data from real-world applications. Students learn to design and critique surveys, observational studies and experiments. The TI-Nspire graphing calculator and both on-line and computer software are utilized to perform simulations, calculate probabilities and examine graphs and probability distributions. This course is designed for the student who is interested in an introduction to data analysis and statistics, but may not be ready for the rigor of AP Statistics. After successful completion of this course, students may go on to AP Statistics for further concept and skill development. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

When advising a student about Technology and Innovation Seminar I, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Technology and Innovation Seminar I belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. Calling all coders, engineers, scientists, artists, writers, and anyone looking to innovate! This course embarks on a transformative, project-based exploration of innovation, computational thinking, and design. Through student-driven exploratory projects, students build skills in computer science, robotics, programming, AI, and design, guided by essential questions around ethics, collaboration, and personal growth. The course employs portfolios to track project questions, challenges, and successes-encouraging metacognition and fostering inclusivity in design thinking. To culminate, students pitch multi-disciplinary projects, leading to an individualized capstone project. Projects might include creating a digital scan of a campus building and creating an AR/VR experience, designing and creating a video game, or using AI to solve world hunger. This course pioneers new modes of expression, applies technology ethically, and opens interdisciplinary opportunities for all future innovators. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
A student comparing Technology and Innovation Seminar I with another Webb class needs more than a label. The advisor should explain that the course appears in Mathematics & Computer Science, summarize the actual catalog description, and separate requirements from recommendations. Calling all coders, engineers, scientists, artists, writers, and anyone looking to innovate! This course embarks on a transformative, project-based exploration of innovation, computational thinking, and design. Through student-driven exploratory projects, students build skills in computer science, robotics, programming, AI, and design, guided by essential questions around ethics, collaboration, and personal growth. The course employs portfolios to track project questions, challenges, and successes-encouraging metacognition and fostering inclusivity in design thinking. To culminate, students pitch multi-disciplinary projects, leading to an individualized capstone project. Projects might include creating a digital scan of a campus building and creating an AR/VR experience, designing and creating a video game, or using AI to solve world hunger. This course pioneers new modes of expression, applies technology ethically, and opens interdisciplinary opportunities for all future innovators.

When advising a student about Technology and Innovation Seminar II, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Technology and Innovation Seminar II belongs to Mathematics & Computer Science. Building on the foundations of innovation and computational thinking established in the first year of the Technology and Innovation seminar, this second-year course challenges students to elevate their skills through greater independence and complexity. Students will engage in self-directed projects exploring computer science, robotics, AI, and design. With minimal guidance, learners will address intricate ethical questions and collaborate on multidisciplinary challenges. Like the first-year course, TI Seminar II will culminate with students pitching their multidisciplinary projects, which will lead to the completion of an individualized capstone project. Students who have taken TI Seminar I can build upon a project they completed during their first year or develop a new project that reflects their interests. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.

Advisors should also help students see how Technology and Innovation Seminar II affects the full-year program. A strong plan accounts for five required courses each semester, possible sixth-course choices, afternoon commitments, and the need to keep the schedule challenging but manageable.
When advising a student about Freshman Seminar, the first step is to name the verified course facts before offering a recommendation. Freshman Seminar belongs to Health & Wellness. All freshmen participate in a required seminar class that focuses on topics in human development, adjustment to boarding school community life and important issues in adolescent physical, social, academic, emotional and personal growth. Classes are kept small to facilitate open, earnest discussion. In addition, all students are required to attend scheduled speakers and discussions through the Health & Living program. The advisor can then discuss workload, interest, readiness, and how the class fits with the student's other courses.
